front: Work: Mugo confesses to betraying the Mau Mau fighter Kihika, who killed District Officer Robson
back: A Grain of Wheat (Ngugi wa Thiong'o)

front: Work: a character breaks his left arm in a foot race on the day of Uhuru (independence)
back: A Grain of Wheat (Ngugi wa Thiong'o)

front: Work: Mumbi becomes pregnant with Karanja's child while husband Gikonyo is in a detention camp
back: A Grain of Wheat (Ngugi wa Thiong'o)

front: Work: General R. fills in when Mugo refuses to give a speech at the independence celebration
back: A Grain of Wheat (Ngugi wa Thiong'o)

front: Work: Munira burns down Wanja's brothel, incidentally killing three brewery directors (Chui, Kimeria, Mzigo)
Work: only a donkey is killed when a plane crashes into the village of Ilmorog
Work: the theng'eta drink brewed from a plant; its potency described by the equation P=3T
Work: title borrowed from Derek Walcott's poem 'The Swamp'; originally titled Ballad of the Barmaid
Work: Abdulla is a one-legged Mau Mau veteran who uses a donkey cart, cares for the orphan Joseph, and leads a march
Work: the Siriana school becomes oppressive after former student Chui returns to lead it
Work: Kiguunda loses his one-and-a-half acre plot of land after being tricked by Ahab and Jezebel into financing a church wedding
back: I Will Marry When I Want (Ngugi wa Thiong'o)
work: I Will Marry When I Want

front: Work: performed at the Kamiriithu Community Centre, which police burned down; this play led to the author's imprisonment
back: I Will Marry When I Want (Ngugi wa Thiong'o)
work: I Will Marry When I Want

front: Work: the title deed framed on the wall repeatedly falls to the ground, foreshadowing its loss
back: I Will Marry When I Want (Ngugi wa Thiong'o)
work: I Will Marry When I Want

front: Work: singers gather singing 'The Satan of poverty must be crushed!'
back: I Will Marry When I Want (Ngugi wa Thiong'o)
work: I Will Marry When I Want

front: Work: a rich woman has a servant fetch water from a pigsty instead of pouring from the jug on the table
back: I Will Marry When I Want (Ngugi wa Thiong'o)
work: I Will Marry When I Want

front: Work: the Ruler of Aburiria orders construction of 'Marching to Heaven'; Kamiti and Nyawira adopt the title wizard persona
back: Wizard of the Crow (Ngugi wa Thiong'o)

front: Work: three ministers have their eyes, ears, and tongues enhanced to better serve their leader
back: Wizard of the Crow (Ngugi wa Thiong'o)

front: Work: the pregnant Ruler gives birth to 'Baby Democracy'
back: Wizard of the Crow (Ngugi wa Thiong'o)

front: Work: every TV screen splits into seven pieces after a club is thrown at a cameraman
back: Wizard of the Crow (Ngugi wa Thiong'o)

front: Work: calls the use of English by African writers a 'cultural bomb'; explains this author's decision to write in Gikuyu
Work: describes how colonial schools punished students for speaking Gikuyu; teachers had students pass around a button
Work: ends with a chapter titled 'The Quest for Relevance'
Work: Remi causes Thoni to commit suicide; Remi is pressured into marrying his brother's widow
Work: written on toilet paper while in prison; a suicidal woman receives an invitation signed by Satan to choose 'seven experts in theft and robbery'
back: Devil on the Cross (Ngugi wa Thiong'o)

front: Work: Waiyaki's failed attempt to unite two Kikuyu villages (Komeno and Makuyu) separated by the river Honia
Author: son Mukoma wa Ngugi wrote Black Star Nairobi and The Rise of the African Novel
Author: co-wrote The Trial of Dedan Kimathi, in which an orange seller helps children smuggle a gun in bread
Author: Mother, Sing for Me (Maitu Njugira) dramatized resistance to the Kipande forced labor system; led to shutdown of his troupe in 1982
Author: The Perfect Nine is a novel-in-verse about 99 suitors competing to become progenitors of nine clans
Author: called George Lamming 'the prime example of peasant revolt in a white-dominated world' in his essay collection Homecoming
